text.title: Solo Launch Day — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every solo launch day must obey. The rules eliminate the canonical first-day failures that every solo founder hits exactly once.

rule.statement: The full smoke-test suite (sign-up, payment, welcome email, support email, password reset, key flows) MUST be executed at T-24h AND T-2h, against the production environment, with results recorded. Skipping either is forbidden.
rule.rationale: A T-24h smoke test catches issues with time to fix; a T-2h smoke test catches anything that broke since (DNS propagation, last-minute deploy, expired cert). Skipping the T-2h test is the canonical "launch reveals a bug from yesterday's deploy" failure.

rule.statement: Stripe (or equivalent payment) MUST be switched to live keys AND verified with a real $1 charge ≥2h before the announce time. Live-key swaps within 2h of announce are forbidden.

rationale.source: Stripe production-deployment best practice; payment-launch postmortems

rule.rationale: Live-key swap is the most failure-prone single operation in a launch. Doing it under time pressure 30 min before announce is how founders end up with Stripe in test mode while emails go out. The 2h buffer absorbs the inevitable hiccup.

rule.statement: Fallback content MUST be ready before launch: holding page if the app goes down, status page URL communicated, refund-and-apology email template, support-canned responses. Fallbacks deployed mid-incident on launch day are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Writing apology copy at 02:00 while the site is down is when the worst comms decisions get made. Pre-written fallbacks reduce the founder's cognitive load to "deploy the prepared response", not "compose a response under stress".

rule.statement: From T-2h to T+12h, NO infrastructure or key changes are made (no deploys, no DNS swaps, no env-var edits) unless triggered by an incident requiring rollback. Improvements wait until T+24h.
rule.rationale: Even small changes (a typo fix, a copy tweak, an env-var update) carry risk that compounds with launch traffic. The change freeze prevents self-inflicted incidents during the highest-traffic window of the quarter.

rule.statement: A documented rollback path that completes in ≤10 minutes MUST exist and have been rehearsed in the T-24h dry run. If the rollback runbook is >10 min OR has never been tested, the launch is NOT ready.

rationale.source: canary / blue-green deployment best practice; field experience

rule.rationale: A 30-minute rollback is functionally never used because by the time the founder considers it, "it might fix itself" wins. A ≤10-minute rollback gets used and limits damage. Untested rollbacks fail at the worst possible moment.

rule.statement: When preconditions in Applies-If hold and no Skip-If trigger applies, walk the procedure end-to-end and run the validator before declaring done.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory run leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The run leaf maps "preconditions met" to the canonical execution path.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the Applies-If preconditions are not met OR any Skip-If disqualifier holds.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory skip leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The skip leaf needs a real rule so non-applicable inputs route cleanly out instead of producing false-positive verdicts.

section.title: Sample launch-day timeline
section.code: T-24h: Full smoke test. Rollback dry-run. Fallback content frozen. Stripe live keys provisioned (not yet swapped). T-2h: Smoke test rerun. Stripe key swap + $1 verify charge. Final scan: monitoring armed, mailer not rate-limited. T-30m: Final breath. Coffee. tg-bot ping armed. T-0: Announce burst (mailing list first, then X / LinkedIn / IndieHackers). Plausible live-counter open. T+15m: Checkpoint: error rate, signup count, first refund? Update launch log. T+1h: Checkpoint: any Sentry spike? Mailer ratelimit? Stripe webhook healthy? Update log. T+4h: Checkpoint: traction signal (signup ratio, conversion to paid). Refresh support inbox. T+12h: Final-day checkpoint. Write closeout note. T+24h: Postmortem: what went right, what to add to next launch's runbook.

example.title: Stripe-key swap done early
example.code:
T-2h: Stripe keys swapped from test to live. $1 test charge from the founder's own card succeeds. Webhook event verified in Stripe dashboard. Test charge refunded. Launch is now payment-ready.
T-0: Mailing list goes out. First customer signs up at T+11min. Charge succeeds visibly in Stripe dashboard. Welcome email delivered.
example.note: Illustrates r2 (Stripe live with buffer). The launch's most-important moment (first paying customer) is undramatic because the payment path was already proven.

example.title: The 9pm key-swap classic
example.code: T+15min: announce burst sent. T+30min: first signup attempt fails — Stripe in test mode. Founder swaps keys hastily, makes a typo, breaks the webhook. Next 40 minutes spent debugging. Mailer queued 80 welcomes with broken activation links. Recovery takes 3 hours of support work.
example.note: r2 violated (no Stripe live pre-launch), r4 violated (key change during launch window), r1 implied (smoke test didn't catch the test-mode state).
